## Model Limitations — What the Gurney Cylinder Assumption Ignores

The Gurney cylinder model is a deliberate simplification. Here is what it omits,
in plain terms:

**1 · The shell is not just a cylinder.**
A real artillery shell has three distinct zones: a pointed nose (ogive), a cylindrical
body, and a flat base. The Gurney *cylinder* formula models only the body. The nose
fragments spray forward at steep upward angles; the base fragments spray backward
and downward. Together these zones account for roughly 20–30 % of the steel mass
but produce none of the fragments in the single-cylinder model. Gold (2007) explicitly
accounts for the tail boom and fuze as separate mass contributors; the one-region
PAFRAG assumption treats the entire shell as a single cylinder, omitting both end
zones (Gold 2017, §2). Consequence: the single-zone (equatorial-belt) field, taken
alone, *underestimates* total fragment count and carries no forward- or rear-sector
hazard.

*Complemented by the four-zone field (§6), not replaced:* the four-zone
decomposition (§6.1) assigns ogive, cylinder, boattail, and base each their own
Mott distribution, Gurney velocity, and spray angle, adding the forward ogive
sector and the rearward base sector. The single-zone belt is **deliberately
retained** alongside it as the drawing-data-free, absolute-magnitude baseline —
the reference the four-zone lobe structure is measured against (three-variant
division of labor, §6). This is a coexisting pair, not an old-to-new
progression. Residual limitations of the zone model are listed in §12.

**2 · Fragments do not all fly in one horizontal plane.**
The cylindrical body does spray predominantly sideways (Figure 4 cross-section), but
even within the cylinder there is an elevation spread around horizontal caused by the
oblique angle at which the detonation wave meets the wall. Gold (2007, Figs. 3 and 9)
shows fragment velocity varying continuously with spray angle ψ; the elevation spread
is roughly ±15°–30°. The v 0.1.0–0.2.0 model put every fragment at exactly θ = 0°
(horizontal), missing fragments that fly over a prone target or clip a standing target
near the head or feet.

*Partially addressed in v 0.3.0:* each zone now sprays at its own elevation angle
(ogive ≈ 80°, boattail ≈ 95°, base ≈ 165°), so fragments are no longer constrained
to horizontal. Within-zone spread (±5°–10° around each zone's midpoint normal) is
still not modelled — see limitation #4.

**3 · One velocity for every fragment.**
Gurney gives a single initial speed V₀ for all fragments, regardless of mass, position
in the shell, or local explosive density. Gold (2007, Fig. 9) shows the measured
fragment velocity distribution spanning roughly ±10–15 % around the Gurney mean. The
Mott distribution already captures mass spread; combining it with a velocity
distribution would widen the lethality envelope at short range and narrow it at long
range. The Gurney constant itself (2 440 m/s for TNT) is a nominal tabulated value;
aging, temperature, and lot-to-lot density variation can shift it by a few percent,
which propagates linearly into V₀ and quadratically into fragment kinetic energy
(no collected source — general explosive engineering).

**4 · Single midpoint spray angle per zone (no elevation spread).**
Each zone is assigned one spray elevation angle (the surface normal at the arc midpoint).
Gold (2017, Fig. 9) shows that fragment velocity varies with polar angle within a zone,
producing an elevation spread of ±5°–10° around the midpoint. This spread is not
modelled; all fragments from a zone are treated as leaving at the same angle. The
effect is smoothed out when the hazard field is integrated over many azimuths, but
peak density near the zone boundary may be slightly overestimated.

**5 · Shell parameters are not sourced from a collected document.**
Wall thickness, projectile mass, and filler mass are taken from US Army TM 9-1901
(cited in code comments) but that manual is not collected in `doc-reference/`.
If these inputs are wrong, every output shifts — V₀ scales with M/C, and μ scales
as r_bu³.

**6 · Mott input uncertainties amplify super-linearly.**
The break-up radius r_bu is derived from the V/V₀ ≈ 3 expansion criterion, which
Gold (2017, §2) describes as "accepted as a rule of thumb for routine one-region
PAFRAG modeling" with only fair agreement to high-speed photography. Steel ductility
variation means harder steel fractures earlier (smaller r_bu, smaller fragments) and
softer steel fractures later; this carries ~10–20 % uncertainty in r_bu. Because
μ ∝ r_bu³ (Gold 2017, eq. 5), a 15 % error in r_bu produces a 50 % error in μ.
Similarly, γ and σ_F each carry ~25 % uncertainty — Mott (1947, Table 1) brackets
γ at 53–67 for steels ranging from 0.2%C to 0.3%C — and both enter μ as the 3/2
power (Gold 2017, eq. 5), giving roughly 35–40 % uncertainty in the fragment size
scale. The R₅₀ sensitivity sweep above captures the σ_F range but not the joint
uncertainty.

**7 · All fragments are tumbling irregular chunks.**
The drag model uses C_D = 0.65, appropriate for a randomly oriented irregular
fragment. Felix et al. (2022, §2.3) document that real fragments are 2–6× longer
than they are wide, and note that shape "has a significant effect on the air
resistance restricting the velocity of the fragment." Some of these elongated splinters
fly with their long axis aligned to the velocity vector (edge-on), producing
C_D ≈ 0.2–0.4 and reaching 20–40 % further than the model predicts. The fraction of
aero-stable splinters is not known from first principles; arena tests suggest it is a
small minority but they dominate the far-field hazard.

**8 · Lethality model assumes no protection and open ground.**
The ES-310 Pk|hit curve (FAS/Navy 1998) is calibrated for standing unprotected
infantry: the 4 kJ "heavy kill" anchor is explicitly described as the
armour-penetrating threshold, meaning the lower anchors (100 J, 1 kJ) apply to bare
flesh only. Three scenarios are unmodelled: (a) body armour reduces torso
vulnerability by a factor of roughly 3–5×; (b) a soldier in a foxhole or behind
cover has only their head and shoulders exposed, reducing presented area by ~80–90 %;
(c) ground ricochet can redirect fragments that would otherwise miss — particularly
relevant at shallow angles of fall. All three effects are bounded and could be added
as scalar multipliers on the presented area w.

**9 · Poisson statistics break down at high fragment density.**
p_kill = 1 − exp(−N_eff) is correct for a single target: if multiple lethal
fragments hit, the target is still dead once, and the formula saturates to 1.
The ES-310 multi-hit formula (FAS/Navy 1998) gives the same per-target result via
Pk = 1 − (1 − Pk|hit)^N_hits. For a *formation*, however, the model computes each
soldier's p_kill independently using the full fragment flux — it ignores depletion
(a fragment that kills soldier A is unavailable to kill soldier B). At close range
(≤ 50 m) with squad-density formations, this overestimates total expected kills.
The fix is a hypergeometric draw over the finite fragment count; it is not needed
for single-target or sparse-formation analysis.

**10 · The fragment distribution is continuous — there are no individual fragments.**
N_eff is computed by integrating the Mott PDF n(m) over mass, treating the fragment
cloud as a smooth density rather than a discrete set of projectiles. This gives the
ensemble-mean outcome over many rounds; a single detonation will produce a finite
count of fragments that scatter around that mean. For the bulk of sub-gram fragments
this is a good approximation — the count is large and the law of large numbers
applies. For the heavy tail (multi-gram fragments, counts in the tens to hundreds),
the discrete nature matters: a single large fragment that happens to land on a target
is a rare but high-consequence event that the mean integral undersells. Monte Carlo
simulation — drawing a finite sample from the Mott distribution and propagating each
fragment individually — would capture this variance. The threshold above which
discrete sampling is preferable to the continuous integral is not yet determined.

**11 · Lethality threshold: kill vs. wound semantics (from A7).**
The ES-310 Pk(KE) curve anchors lethality at 100 J (10% kill), 1 kJ (50% kill), and
4 kJ (90% kill), calibrated against pistol-calibre rounds on a uniformed soldier.
British WW2 research (1941) established that ~1 g splinters require only ~6.8 J to
produce an incapacitating wound in unprotected tissue — roughly 15× below the ES-310
10% floor. The two thresholds measure different outcomes:

| Source              | Outcome modelled                         | ~50% threshold (1 g fragment) |
| ------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| British 1941        | Wound (skin penetration, bare flesh)     | ~6.8 J                        |
| German Rohne        | Incapacitation (uniformed soldier)       | ~80 J                         |
| ES-310 (this model) | Kill (remove from combat)                | ~1 000 J                      |

Consequence: fragments below ~100 J are assigned Pk = 0. A 1 g fragment carries 100 J
only out to ~90 m slant range; the British data suggest it wounds to ~250 m. Since
the Mott distribution peak for a 105 mm shell is near 0.3 g (by count), the model's
lethality estimate is carried almost entirely by the multi-gram tail and will
understate area effect relative to WW2 casualty data if "wound" is the operational
criterion. See §9.2 for the Sturdivan tissue-penetration model as a future replacement.

**12 · Four-zone model assumptions (v 0.3.0).**

- **Tier-2 default CRH = 6.0** for shells without drawing-derived ogive arc
  geometry is an *engineering convention*, not sourced from any document
  in `doc-reference/`. CRH = 6 is representative of WW2 HE shells (M1 is
  6.02, M107 is 6.74); the resulting spray angle is in the [75°, 88°]
  band but the precise value carries ~ ±3° uncertainty.
- **Base-zone spray angle θ^b = 165°** is phenomenological. No primary
  source in `doc-reference/` resolves the rearward fragment cone angle of
  a thick base plate driven by axial detonation rarefaction. The value is
  consistent with the qualitative "strongly rearward but not exactly 180°
  due to finite base diameter" observation in NWC TP 7124 but is not
  literature-anchored.
- **Posture box-body dimensions** ($w_\perp, h, d$ for STANDING and PRONE)
  are NATO casualty-modelling engineering convention. The canonical
  references — Cunniff (2014, Int. Symp. Ballistics) and AEP-55 Vol. 3 —
  are **not** present in `doc-reference/`. Treat absolute posture-resolved
  hit counts as ±25 % engineering estimates until the references are
  collected.
- **$R_{50}$ recalibration pending.** The geometry-factor change from the
  width-based $s^{-1}$ form to the area-based $s^{-2}$ form in eq. (22')
  changes absolute hit counts: the new model is *smaller* by a factor of
  roughly $w_\perp h/(w \cdot s)$, i.e. of order $1.7/s$ (see
  target-area-profile derivation §4.3). The calibrated $R_{50}$ values
  must be re-anchored against TM 9-1901 lethal-area data once the new
  factor is in place. Until then, **absolute** lethal radii from the
  four-zone field are unreliable; *relative* zone contributions and
  lobed structure are valid.
- **Base equivalent-column $C^b_\text{eff}$** convention (derivation
  §3.3) is a geometric approximation, not literature-sourced.
- **AoF-resolved validation data** is not present in any of BRL 126,
  NWC TP 7124, or SAND92-0243. The four-zone lobed structure is a
  qualitative prediction (forward density > rearward at AoF < 90°) that
  cannot yet be checked against empirical fragment-density-vs-azimuth
  data.
- **The single-zone − four-zone difference map is not a clean zone
  attribution.** The sensitivity app's "Four-zone − Single-zone" diff
  (§6) is *grid-exact* (both fields share the same grid, so no
  interpolation error enters), but the single-zone baseline is the legacy
  belt path (`_expected_kills_3d_point`), which uses a **backward**
  shell-axis convention ($\hat{e}=(-\cos\alpha,0,-\sin\alpha)$) and a
  different off-boresight normalization than the four-zone forward-axis
  path. The two agree by construction only on the equatorial belt /
  boresight plane; off it the diff conflates the genuine end-zone
  contribution with a residual, unreconciled axis-convention and
  normalization mismatch (belt-axis note,
  `updates/lethal-fragment-density-field/derivation.md` §5.4). It should
  be read as an indicator of directional (fore/aft-lobe) structure, not as
  an exact per-zone lethality attribution.
- **Hard-cutoff spray belts create unphysical dead zones between zone
  boundaries.** Each zone's contribution to a ground point is gated by a
  ±15° (``delta_deg``) rect-function band around the zone's nominal spray
  angle. Ground positions falling between belts — the ≈ 0°–65° nose shadow
  and the ≈ 110°–150° gap between boattail and base — receive zero fragment
  density, appearing as white regions in the hazard map. In reality the
  spray distribution is smooth and continuous (Gold 2017, Fig. 9). The
  fix is to replace the hard acceptance test with a smooth angular weight
  (e.g., Gaussian in polar angle), which also correctly handles overlap
  between adjacent zones.
- **Fragment acceptance test targets ground impact (z = 0), not body
  intercept.** A fragment is accepted for a ground cell only if its
  downward velocity component satisfies $v_{gz} < 0$, i.e.\
  $\sin(\mathrm{AoF} + \theta_z) > 0$. Fragments with $v_{gz} \geq 0$
  are discarded regardless of whether they could strike a person at
  torso or head height.

  The effect is most severe at steep angles of fall. At AoF = 90° the
  cylinder zone ($\theta_z = 90°$) sprays exactly horizontally
  ($v_{gz} = 0$) and is excluded entirely; the model therefore shows a
  false safe zone of several metres around the burst point. A standing
  soldier at 2 m would in reality be struck at chest height by these
  horizontal fragments. For prone targets the effect is smaller (centre
  of mass ≈ 0.3 m) but non-zero at close range.

  The correct fix is to test whether the fragment ray intersects a target
  volume spanning $[0,\, h_\text{person}]$ in height rather than the
  $z = 0$ plane alone. Until that is implemented, P(kill) close to the
  burst at steep AoF (≳ 70°) is underestimated for standing targets, and
  the "inner safe ring" visible in the sensitivity app at AoF = 90° is a
  model artefact, not real protection.
